picks.narrative: A 11.2pp gap: IBP has WSH at 55.3%, the market at 43.2%. The largest lift is pitcher quality (xfip) (~+1.1pp): Andrew Alvarez 2.74 xFIP vs Payton Tolle 3.94 xFIP. The main pushback is defense (oaa) (~-0.6pp): BOS defense grades +27 OAA better on the season — a run-prevention headwind. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +127 still sits inside the playable range (to -105). The win condition is five-plus quality innings from Andrew Alvarez and avoiding early bullpen exposure.

picks.narrative: Model and market disagree on TEX by 7.8pp — 55.2% fair against a 47.9% price. The model isn't leaning on a marquee matchup here — no single baseball factor rises to a headline; the disagreement is with the posted price itself. The main pushback is home field (~-1.1pp): CLE's home-field context weighs against the pick playing on the road. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current +111 still sits inside the playable range (to -105). The read here is the price, not the profile — the model just makes this a better side than the posted number does.

picks.narrative: The model sees PIT as a 49.3% side; the market is pricing 44.6%, leaving a 4.8pp edge. There's no standout starter, lineup, or bullpen signal driving this — the gap comes from the model's baseline read of the side, not a visible matchup edge. The main pushback is home field (~-1.1pp): PHI's home-field context weighs against the pick playing on the road. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current +125 still sits inside the playable range (to +121). This is mostly a price play — the model likes the number more than the matchup, so the baseball case is mixed.

picks.narrative: IBP values KC 4.5pp higher than the market — 47.3% model vs 42.8% implied. Several smaller signals stack the same way — lineup quality (~+0.9pp), defense (oaa) (~+0.6pp) — with no single one dominating. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-0.8pp): Seth Lugo 5.18 xFIP vs Shane McClanahan 4.39 xFIP. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +134 still sits inside the playable range (to +131). As a cluster edge rather than one dominant mismatch, it is a thinner, more fragile signal — worth sizing accordingly.

picks.narrative: The model prices STL at 48.9% to win where the no-vig market implies 44.6%, a 4.4pp disagreement. No individual baseball factor clears the bar for a headline here — this is not a single visible mismatch, it is the model pricing the side differently than the market. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current +125 still sits inside the playable range (to +123). This isn't a clean all-signal agreement — it's the model calling the posted number too generous for the true win probability.

picks.narrative: The model prices COL at 45.6% to win where the no-vig market implies 41.3%, a 4.3pp disagreement. The largest lift is home field (~+1.1pp): Home-park and 2026 home-game context add a modest edge for COL. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-1.1pp): Kyle Freeland 4.79 xFIP vs Max Meyer 3.59 xFIP. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +142 still sits inside the playable range (to +140). The edge is the model pricing COL differently than the market rather than one of the marquee starter/lineup/bullpen buckets — worth taking at the number, not chasing past it.

picks.narrative: IBP makes MIL 63.1% fair versus a 59.1% no-vig market price — a 4.1pp gap. Several smaller signals stack the same way — pitcher quality (xfip) (~+1.1pp), home field (~+0.9pp) — with no single one dominating. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current -144 still sits inside the playable range (to -145). The case is a stack of small same-direction signals, not one dominant mismatch — treat it as the thinner edge it is.
